title: Nehemiah 11
subtitle: Repopulation of Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: They "cast lots" (gōrāl) to bring one in ten to live in Jerusalem. The lot is not chance but divine appointment (Prov 16:33). Those who volunteered (hanniddəḇîm) beyond the lot were "blessed" by the people — voluntary sacrifice honoured.

refs.note: "The city was large but there were few people in it" — the problem this chapter solves.
"The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD."

notes.note: One in ten: a tithe of the population. The same principle as the tithe of income (10:37–38). The community tithes its people as well as its produce.
"The people commended all who volunteered." The volunteers receive public honour. Moving to Jerusalem meant leaving ancestral farmland for an urban life — a real sacrifice.

Calvin: "Those who volunteered were blessed because they chose the harder path without compulsion. Voluntary sacrifice is the highest form of obedience.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "the holy city" (haʿîr haqqoḏeš) is used for the first time here. Jerusalem’s holiness is the theological reason for its repopulation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson notes the lot-casting parallels the original land distribution under Joshua (Josh 14–19). Repopulating Jerusalem recapitulates the conquest. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "A tithe of persons. The principle is the same as the tithe of goods: the first portion belongs to God. Jerusalem is God’s city; it needs God’s people.". Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

hist.context: The wall is built (ch 6), the covenant sealed (ch 10), but Jerusalem is empty (7:4). The final step: populating the city. One in ten chosen by lot; others volunteer. The combination of compulsion and voluntarism shows practical wisdom — relying solely on volunteers would leave the city short.

sections.header: Verses 3–36 — Residents by Tribe and Settlements Outside Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: The residents (yōšəḇê) are listed by tribal origin: Judah, Benjamin, priests, Levites, gatekeepers. The categories mirror Ezra 2 and Nehemiah 7 — the community consistently defines itself by these classifications.

hist.historical: The population list includes 468 Judahites, 928 Benjaminites, 1,192 priests, 284 Levites, and 172 gatekeepers — approximately 3,044 named personnel plus families. The total population of Jerusalem may have been 10,000–15,000. The outlying settlements (vv.25–36) show the restored community occupying a small fraction of pre-exilic Judah.
hist.context: The settlement list (vv.25–36) traces Judahite and Benjaminite villages from Beersheba to the Hinnom Valley. The geography defines the restored province: a small territory centered on Jerusalem, nothing like David’s kingdom. The community is modest in size but specific in identity.

refs.note: The original tribal allotments — the ancestral claim these settlers are reasserting.
The parallel settlement list from Chronicles — covering the same categories of residents (priests, Levites, temple servants, gatekeepers) in Jerusalem after the return from exile.
The original tribal distribution by lot — the same method used in Nehemiah 11:1 to determine which families would resettle in the underpopulated capital city.

notes.note: The categories are consistent: laypeople (Judah, Benjamin), priests, Levites, gatekeepers. Every tier is needed. A city needs more than walls; it needs a functioning religious and civic structure.
The Levites on duty in Jerusalem include "the overseer of the Levites" and a royal appointee "in all affairs relating to the people." Both religious and civil administration.
Settlements from Beersheba to the Valley of Hinnom — the traditional boundaries of Judah, though the actual territory was much smaller than the pre-exilic kingdom.
"Some Levites of Judah settled in Benjamin" — tribal mixing for practical reasons. The post-exilic community is less rigidly tribal than its pre-exilic predecessor.

Calvin: "Every village is a witness to God’s faithfulness. They returned not to an abstract homeland but to specific places with names and histories.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET identifies most of the listed towns, noting they cluster in the Judean hills and Shephelah — the agricultural heartland of the restored province. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

williamson.source: H.G.M. Williamson, Ezra, Nehemiah, WBC (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Williamson compares this list with 1 Chr 9:2–17, finding significant overlap. Both may derive from a census document of the early Persian period.
Williamson maps the settlements and concludes the restored province was approximately 25 miles north-south and 15 miles east-west — tiny by pre-exilic standards.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Ezra and Nehemiah, TOTC (1979) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "The village names read like a homecoming list. Each name is a place reclaimed, a heritage recovered. The land promise, on however small a scale, is being honoured.". Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: The geography defines the restored province: a small territory centered on Jerusalem, nothing like David’s kingdom…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNow the leaders of the people settled in Jerusalem. The rest of the people cast lots to bring one out of every ten of them to live in Jerusalem, the holy city, while the remaining nine were to stay in their own towns.ESVNow the leaders of the people lived in Jerusalem. And the rest of the people cast lots to bring one out of ten to live in Jerusalem the holy city, while nine out of ten remained in the other towns.NIVFrom the descendants of Benjamin: Sallu son of Meshullam, the son of Joed, the son of Pedaiah, the son of Kolaiah, the son of Maaseiah, the son of Ithiel, the son of Jeshaiah,ESVAnd these are the sons of Benjamin: Sallu the son of Meshullam, son of Joed, son of Pedaiah, son of Kolaiah, son of Maaseiah, son of Ithiel, son of Jeshaiah,

rec.text: "One in ten: a tithe of the population. The same principle as the tithe of income (10:37–38). The community tithes its people as well as its produce."

rows.label: The Residents — 11:3–24
rows.text: Every tier: Judah, Benjamin, priests, Levites, gatekeepers.

rows.label: The Settlements — 11:25–36
rows.text: Village names reclaimed. The land promise on a small scale.

hebtext.note: They "cast lots" (gōrāl) to bring one in ten to live in Jerusalem. The lot is not chance but divine appointment (Prov 16:33). Those who volunteered (hanniddəḇîm) beyond the lot were "blessed" by the people — voluntary sacrifice honoured.

The residents (yōšəḇê) are listed by tribal origin: Judah, Benjamin, priests, Levites, gatekeepers. The categories mirror Ezra 2 and Nehemiah 7 — the community consistently defines itself by these classifications.

thread.text: "The city was large but there were few people in it" — the problem this chapter solves.
"The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD."
The original tribal allotments — the ancestral claim these settlers are reasserting.

themes.note: Holiness and sovereignty share emphasis: Jerusalem is "the holy city" and the lot is God’s instrument.

coaching.tip: The people cast lots to determine who will live in Jerusalem — one in ten. Living in the holy city is presented as a sacrifice, not a privilege. The city needs population, and the 'volunteers' are commended. Sometimes faithfulness means moving where the need is, not where comfort is.

prayer_prompt: Leaders lived in Jerusalem; others cast lots to repopulate the city. Consider asking God about your willingness to live where needs are greatest — not just where comfort is.
